narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped—specifically, a 55.6% chance of not passing on the first try. Picture your profile coming together like chapters in a story: the strongest signal was your Year 3 decile of 1, placing you at the very bottom of your class academically that year, which painted the clearest picture of struggle ahead. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 2, showing those early grades weren't quite the launchpad stronger performers had, pulling the outlook firmly toward difficulty. Your LSAT score of 36, while respectable, didn't stand out enough in a field of high achievers to counterbalance those class rankings, adding another layer of concern. Coming from a middle-income family positioned you solidly but without the extra resources that sometimes help others bridge gaps, making this one of the key factors tipping toward failure. Your background as a white male and full-time student status fit common patterns, but in this mix, they nudged the prediction downward rather than lifting it. Even your undergraduate GPA of 2.8, though not disastrous, wove in as another thread suggesting the academic rigor of law school and the bar might prove an uphill battle. It's this combination—bottom deciles in key years, a mid-tier LSAT, and supportive but not standout circumstances—that built the model's story of caution, leading to the tough call on your application. You're not defined by these numbers, though; many have rewritten their stories with targeted improvements. If you boost those class rankings or retake the LSAT, doors could open wider next time. Hang in there—this is just one page, not the whole book.
